title: Salted caramel sauce
subtitle: Dry caramel dissolved in hot cream with a generous pinch of flaked salt
excerpt: A dry caramel sauce made without adding water to the sugar. The sugar caramelises cleanly in the pan, then hot cream is poured in and the butter added off the heat. The result is a rich, pourable sauce that keeps in the fridge for two weeks and uses on ice cream, cakes, and sticky puddings.
sourceNotes: Caramel sauce from cooked sugar and cream is documented in British and French domestic confectionery guides from the nineteenth century. The technique of making a dry caramel (without water) for a cleaner, more even colour appears in patisserie texts and is a straightforward adaptation of standard caramel methods documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and similar public-domain domestic references. The addition of flaked salt as a balance to the sweetness is a widely reproduced modern technique now standard across domestic baking literature.

content.text: Hot caramel is significantly hotter than boiling water and sticks to skin. Add the cream carefully, the mixture will bubble vigorously. Keep children out of the kitchen during this stage.

Dry caramel is faster than wet but leaves less room for error. Spread the sugar in an even layer so it melts uniformly. Watch the colour closely in the last minute; the difference between amber and burnt is about 10 seconds.
Spread the {{caster-sugar}} evenly across the base of a medium saucepan. Place over medium heat. Do not stir. The
will begin to melt at the edges after 2 to 3 minutes. Swirl the pan gently to even out the melting. When all the sugar is liquid and a rich amber (not dark brown), remove from the heat.

Stand back and pour the warm {{double-cream}} into the caramel in a steady stream. It will boil up violently. Stir with the silicone spatula until the bubbling subsides and the sauce is smooth. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} off the heat and stir until melted. Stir in the {{sea-salt-flakes}}. Pour into a jar and leave to cool.
